banners.name: Vinaya · Discipline
banners.def: True spiritual teaching requires both a readiness to listen and the humility to surrender one's ego to the process of learning and transformation.

banners.name: Vinaya · Discipline
banners.def: Discipline is the willing alignment of thought, word, and deed toward genuine self-improvement under proper guidance.

banners.name: False Wisdom · Pandita without Sambuddha
banners.def: Intellectual learning without inner awakening and corresponding character transformation produce no spiritual benefit.

banners.name: Bad Character · Dusshil
banners.def: Poor character makes a person universally despised, regardless of external status, robes, or intellectual accomplishments.

banners.name: Vice over Virtue
banners.def: Choosing temporary sensory pleasures over lasting virtue is spiritually self-destructive — like a cook who throws away nourishing grain.

banners.name: Atma-Hit · Self-Welfare
banners.def: The entire spiritual path is rooted in rational self-interest: you practice because it genuinely benefits you.

banners.name: Seela · Good Character
banners.def: The wise disciple who establishes discipline and good character earns genuine respect everywhere.

banners.name: Satsang · Company of the Wise
banners.def: Proximity to wisdom is valuable only when you absorb what is genuinely transformative, not what merely entertains.

banners.name: Kshama · Patience with Correction
banners.def: The wise person cultivates patience as an inner practice, recognizing that receiving correction without anger is foundational to growth.

banners.name: Deceit and Idle Speech
banners.def: Deception corrupts inner clarity and outer relationships; excessive talking scatters mental energy and generates harmful karma.

banners.name: Non-Repetition of Error
banners.def: A single mistake becomes a choice when repeated, a habit when continued — interrupt the cycle at first repetition.

banners.name: Stubborn Resistance to Guidance
banners.def: The truly wise respond to light correction without needing repeated blows — quick receptivity marks genuine intelligence.

banners.name: Guru's Apparent Harshness
banners.def: A guru's firm corrections arise from compassion and desire to transform the student — understanding this builds unshakeable devotion.

banners.name: Right Speech · Vak-Samiti
banners.def: Silence when unasked, complete truth when asked, freedom from anger in all speech—these together constitute genuine self-restraint.

banners.name: Atma-Daman · Self-Mastery
banners.def: The self alone must tame itself — but the one who succeeds becomes an unconditional refuge for all beings.

banners.name: Tapa · Self-Chosen Discipline
banners.def: True strength lies in discipline chosen by yourself through conscious effort, never in being forced by external circumstances.

banners.name: Eka-Bhava · Public-Private Alignment
banners.def: Discipline and respect for the wise must be consistent in private as in public — serving only when watched is pretense.

banners.name: Physical Conduct · Bodily Restraint
banners.def: Even the way you sit and position your body communicates respect or disrespect — physical discipline is inseparable from inner discipline.

banners.name: Proper Physical Comportment
banners.def: Control of body posture, gesture, and movement shows mastery over oneself and respect for the sacred teaching and teacher.

banners.name: Receptive Silence · Sravana
banners.def: When elders speak, silence and reverent attention are not passivity but active receptivity—the condition for true learning.

banners.name: Disrespect Toward the Teacher
banners.def: Disrespect, whether expressed through words, tone, or actions, severs the transmission of wisdom that requires trust and reverence.

banners.name: Proper Inquiry · Humble Questioning
banners.def: To ask in the right way—standing with folded hands, free from arrogance—opens the teacher's heart to share deeper wisdom.

banners.name: Guru's Complete Teaching
banners.def: The wise teacher, when asked properly, offers both the scripture and its living meaning—connecting words to transformative understanding.

banners.name: Falsehood and Harsh Speech
banners.def: Falsehood is the cardinal sin of speech; harsh words wound the listener and corrupt the speaker's inner clarity.

banners.name: Sinful and Prideful Speech
banners.def: Words spoken from pride, for selfish gain, or without consideration of their harm generate karma that binds the soul.

banners.name: Proper Celibacy · Brahmacharya
banners.def: The monk maintains appropriate distance from the opposite sex—not from shame, but from recognition that unguarded contact generates binding karma.

banners.name: Openness to All Teaching
banners.def: Whatever the wise teach—whether gently or harshly—the sincere student receives it as beneficial and works to integrate it.

banners.name: Resistance to Correction · Unwisdom
banners.def: The unwise mistake harsh teaching for harm and reject the very medicine they need — this closes the door to growth.

banners.name: Compassion in Teaching
banners.def: What appears harsh is actually compassion—the wise teacher delivers what is truly beneficial, even if it comes with severity.

banners.name: Humility in Physical Presence
banners.def: Sitting on a humble, stable seat and occupying minimal space communicates inner humility before the teacher and the teaching.

banners.name: Timeliness · Right Action at Right Time
banners.def: The disciplined one learns the proper timing for all activities—alms-collection, return, rest, practice—and maintains this rhythm.

banners.name: Mindful Conduct in Alms-Collection
banners.def: Even in the practical act of begging, the monk maintains full awareness and propriety, never lingering or deviating from right conduct.

banners.name: Celibacy and Right Distance
banners.def: Maintaining proper distance from the opposite sex and keeping any necessary conversation brief protects the monk's inner focus.

banners.name: Madhya-Marga · The Middle Way
banners.def: In all things—distance, position, behavior—the wise one acts with balance and moderation, never extreme in any direction.

banners.name: Solitude for Practice
banners.def: In a peaceful, undisturbed place with few living beings, the monk mediates diligently—solitude is essential for inner work.

banners.name: Conviction · Removing Doubt
banners.def: Having learned well from the wise, the sage establishes that learning firmly within and abandons all doubt and uncertainty.

banners.name: Receptivity and Stubbornness
banners.def: The wise respond like a good horse to gentle guidance, while the foolish must be driven like a stubborn donkey—be swift in learning.

banners.name: Harsh Teaching as Compassion
banners.def: When the teacher scolds, strikes, or reproaches, the wise disciple understands this is for their own welfare and transformation.

banners.name: Guru as Parent
banners.def: The teacher holds the student with parental love and teaches with the intention of their liberation—this is the basis of genuine devotion.

banners.name: Anger at the Teacher
banners.def: Anger toward the teacher severs the lifeline of transmission—the wise maintain equanimity and trust, even when uncomfortable.

banners.name: Devotional Appeasement
banners.def: When the teacher is displeased, the sincere disciple returns with folded hands, faith, and confession—this gesture itself heals the relationship.

banners.name: Right Conduct Prevents Reproof
banners.def: One who learns and practices right conduct from the wise teacher never faces reproof—their conduct itself becomes their shield.

banners.name: Attunement to the Guru
banners.def: Understanding the teacher's wishes through their mind and words, the disciple fulfills them with respect and intelligence.

banners.name: Faithful Service
banners.def: Without needing to be prompted, and having been properly guided once, the wise one performs all tasks diligently and faithfully.

banners.name: Humility and Renown
banners.def: The wise one who knows and bows with genuine humility gains fame in the world and becomes a refuge for all beings.

banners.name: Guru's Blessings
banners.def: Those whose teachers are pleased and who are praised by the wise gain abundant means and prosperity in both realms.

banners.name: Integrated Spiritual Practice
banners.def: One who is well-disciplined, steady in mind, established in meditation and austerity, becomes radiant with spiritual accomplishment.

banners.name: Moksha · Final Liberation
banners.def: Worshipped by gods and humans, one who abandons the body of impurity through complete discipline attains the eternal state of liberation.
